observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Human Resource Management Officer to immediately adopt the Automated Teller Machine (ATM) payroll system to safeguard government funds from the risk of loss or misapplication associated with the manual payroll system.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken.
observations.reason: The Municipality explained that they encountered challenges in scheduling the application and processing of JO personnel to the bank as most were assigned in the field and outside the Municipal Hall.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instructs the Municipal Accountant to
record the purchases of supplies and materials for stock as inventory following Perpetual
Inventory Method as prescribed under Section 114(1) of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the OIC Head, Municipal GSO
to submit the required RPCI to the Municipal Accountant and the Audit Team not later
than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters,
respectively.

Additionally, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant and OIC Head, Municipal GSO to conduct periodic reconciliation of
property and accounting records to ensure that inventories are accurately recorded
and reported.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and
Municipal Treasurer to expedite and facilitate the request for a write-off for the
dormant Other Receivables and Loans Receivables accounts in accordance with COA
Circular No. 2023-008.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Officer-in-Charge (OIC)
Head, Municipal GSO to:

a. Ensure the proper maintenance of updated, complete and accurate PC for each
category of PPE, covering all the properties of the Municipality, to strengthen
internal controls and facilitate accurate monitoring of acquisition, issuances,
transfers, and disposals; and

b. Implement a systematic process for preparing, issuing and regularly updating
ARE to establish and maintain clear accountability over all issued PPE.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct
the:

a. OIC Head, Municipal GSO to submit a complete IIRUP for all unserviceable
properties; and

b. Property Appraisal and Disposal Committee to expedite the disposal of the
unserviceable properties in accordance with the existing rules and regulations, to
mitigate risks of loss, exposure to harmful elements, further deterioration, and a
reduction in potential salvage values, as well as to maximize other benefits.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal GSO to:

a. Prepare and maintain the prescribed forms, registry, and reports such as SPC,
RegSPI, RSPI, RLSDDSP and IIRUSP, for the establishment of proper control and
accountability over the semi-expendable properties; and

b. Report in the RPCSP all semi-expendable properties including those owned by
the Municipal from the previous years with complete details such as property
number and unit value.

In addition, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to maintain the SPLC to record the acquisition, description, custody,
impairment, issue/transfer/disposal, repair history, and other information about the
property.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:

a. Require the Municipal Accountant and the BAC Chairperson to submit all
supporting documents listed under item 9.1.3.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001,
along with the missing documents not attached to the contract, as well as other
required documentation; and

b. Instruct a thorough review of the ABC and direct the Municipal Environment and
Natural Resources Office (MENRO) to prepare accurate and well-justified
estimates. This will ensure that the most advantageous price for the government
is obtained.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Human Resource
Management Office (HRMO) and Municipal Accountant to submit the approved contracts
of the 69 JO personnel and henceforth, ensure submission along with the disbursement
vouchers of the first claim to enable the timely review and evaluation for effective
communication of audit results.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Provide Allowance for Impairment for the assessed/evaluated non-collectible receivables; and
b. Facilitate write-off for the noted dormant and non-collectible loans receivables by conducting the necessary procedures prescribed in Item 7.0 of COA Circular No. 2023-008.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer already submitted an updated schedule of aging of receivables and assess/evaluate the collectability of the noted dormant receivables by completing the ongoing update on the inventory of borrowers.
The Municipal Accountant already prepared the necessary adjusting entries to provide Allowance for Impairment for the assessed/evaluated non-collectible receivables.
Management is still securing necessary documents to support the filing of request for authority to write-off the dormant accounts.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant initially identified few individuals who were already deceased. Moreover, some were not applied for write-off as they are in the process of creating a loan reconstruction program for the borrowers.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue a memorandum addressed to the AOs/employees on the timely liquidation of CAs with complete documentary requirements within the prescribed period, consistent with the pertinent provisions of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Refrain from granting additional/new CA to AOs with unliquidated CAs; 
b. Ensure that previous CAs are liquidated before granting of new ones evidenced by Certification from the Municipal Accountant that previous CAs have been fully liquidated and accounted for in the books; and
c. Ensure that unexpended balance of CA for assistance to individuals/families in crisis situation (AICS) is returned/refunded by the concerned AO in her liquidation report before granting new CA.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Mayor issued Memorandum No. 19-2024 dated May 20, 2024 regarding the timely liquidation of CA and the corresponding sanctions for failure to liquidate the CA within the prescribed period, consistent with the pertinent provisions of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002.
The Municipal Accountant implemented that previous CAs are first liquidated before granting of new ones as evidenced by attached Certification starting June 2024.
Moreover, the balance of CA for AICS were already returned and refunded when not fully distributed before granting of new ones.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer, include in the annual budget sufficient appropriation for insurance expenses to ensure that all insurable property are covered by property insurance with the General Service Insurance Fund of the GSIS, in compliance with the pertinent provisions of RA No. 656 and COA Circular No. 92-390.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer, included appropriation for insurance expenses in the annual budget of CY 2024.
observations.reason: Although the Municipality already included appropriation for insurance expenses in the annual budget of CY 2024, the properties were not applied for insurance with the GSIS.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) and Municipal Engineer:
a. Submit written justification on the underutilization of the fund and detailed status of each project as of April 30, 2024 to the Audit Team; and
b. Effectively and judiciously plan the priority development projects to be included in the Annual Investment Program (AIP) by indicating specific schedule for each procurement activity throughout the year and considering its feasibility and necessity and which could be strictly implemented for a given timeframe.
We also recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and Municipal Engineer to effectively plan the development projects in the AIP to be responsive to the priority needs of the constituents.
observations.managementAction: The MPDC and Municipal Engineer submitted their written justification specifying that they encountered delays in the implementation of the development projects due to various challenges such as right of way, engineering restrictions, among others. Moreover, the submitted AIP already reported a specific timeline for effective and efficient implementation of projects. Further, the current appropriation for LDF was optimally 76% for CY 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (MDRRMO) to:
a. Refrain from charging the salaries and wages of the noted Job Order (JO) personnel against the Mitigation Fund, instead source them from the appropriations for Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (MOOE);
b. Consider the exclusion of the noted maintenance activities in the LDRRMFIP and include PPAs that can contribute long-term effects in the disaster prevention and mitigation capacity of the Municipality; and
c. Judiciously plan the implementation of planned PPAs in line with the timeframe indicated in the LDRRMFIP to maximize the utilization of the fund for the attainment of the objective of building a disaster resilient community.
observations.managementAction:
The MDRRMO decreased the charging of salaries of the JO personnel charged against the Mitigation Fund.
The MDRRMO conducted a meeting with the Municipal Budget Officer for possible exclusion of the noted projects of similar maintenance nature and considered necessary as allowed in Section 5.1.8 of NDRRMCDBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1.
The MDRRMO started to maintain a monitoring report for the utilization and implementation of PPAs in the LDRRMFIP starting CY 2025.
observations.reason:
Management assessed that the noted expenses fall within the scope of “other programs or projects of similar nature” and considered necessary as allowed in Section 5.1.8 of NDRRMCDBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1.
The observation recurred in CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) to immediately execute contracts between the Municipality and the JO personnel specifying their defined functions and duration of service. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the employment of JO personnel is covered with the contract of service to protect the interest of both the personnel and the Municipality in accordance with the pertinent provisions of CSC Resolution No. 02-0790.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-Municipal HRMO prepared and executed contracts with defined functions and duration of service for the JO Personnel hired last July 2024 and copies of the executed contracts were submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and
Municipal Treasurer to prepare schedule of aging of receivables and
assess/evaluate the collectability of the noted dormant receivables by completing
the ongoing update on the inventory of borrowers.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
to:

a. provide Allowance for Impairment for the assessed/evaluated non-collectible
receivables, in compliance with the pertinent provisions of IPSAS 29; and

b. facilitate request for write-off for the noted dormant and non-collectible loans
receivables by conducting the necessary procedures prescribed in Item 7.0 of
COA Circular No. 2023-008.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue a memorandum addressed to the
AOs/employees on the timely liquidation of CAs with complete documentary
requirements within the prescribed period, consistent with the pertinent provisions
of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
to:

a. refrain from granting additional/new CA to AOs with unliquidated CAs;

b. ensure that previous CAs are liquidated before granting of new ones evidenced
by Certification from the Municipal Accountant that previous CAs have been
fully liquidated and accounted for in the books; and

c. ensure that unexpended balance of CA for AICS is returned/refunded by the
concerned AO in her liquidation report before granting new CA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal General Services
Officer to immediately facilitate the insurance of the noted property with the GSIS
under the General Service Insurance Fund.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the Municipal
Budget Officer, include in the annual budget sufficient appropriation for insurance
expenses to ensure that all insurable property are covered by property insurance
with the General Service Insurance Fund of the GSIS, in compliance with the
pertinent provisions of RA No. 656 and COA Circular No. 92-390.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to:
• submit written justification on the underutilization of the fund and provide the status
of each project as of April 30, 2024 to the Audit Team; and
• effectively and judiciously plan the priority development projects to be included in
the Annual Investment Program (AIP) by indicating specific schedule for each
procurement activity throughout the year and considering its feasibility and
necessity and which could be strictly implemented for a given timeframe.

b. Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and Municipal Engineer to
effectively plan the development projects in the AIP to be responsive to the priority
needs of the constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Officer to:

a. refrain from charging the salaries and wages of the noted JO personnel against
the Mitigation Fund, instead source them from the appropriations for
MOOE; and

b. consider the exclusion of the noted maintenance activities in the LDRRMFIP and
include PPAs that can contribute long-term effects in the disaster prevention
and mitigation capacity of the Municipality; and

c. judiciously plan the implementation of planned PPAs in line with the timeframe
indicated in the LDRRMFIP to maximize the utilization of the fund for the
attainment of the objective of building a disaster resilient community.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the OIC-Municipal
Human Resources Management Officer execute contracts between the Municipality
and the JO personnel specifying their defined functions and duration of service.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the employment of JO
personnel is covered with the contract of service to protect the interest of both the
personnel and the Municipality in accordance with the pertinent provisions of CSC
Resolution No. 02-0790.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Human
Resource Management Officer to immediately adopt the automated teller machine
payroll system to safeguard government funds from the risk of loss or misapplication
associated with the manual payroll system.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to disclose the required financial information in the Notes to Financial Statements in the ensuing year to assist users of financial statements in the full understanding of the accounts presented thereof pursuant to IPSAS 1 and other relevant accounting rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant disclosed the required financial information in the Notes to Financial Statements in CY 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Municipal General Services Officer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records, pursuant to COA Circular 2020-006; b. Municipal General Services Officer to prepare and submit • List of PPEs found at station using Annex B template of the Circular; • List of Non-Existing/Missing PPEs, with complete information on which PPEs could not be produced upon demand and those which have pending Requests for Relief, as well as the corresponding accountable officers/personnel in Annex C of the Circular; and • Updated/corrected Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) including those found at station but considered unserviceable. c. Municipal Accountant to: • update their records and prepare necessary adjustments in the books of accounts relative to the PPE items found at the station and non-existing/missing PPE items in Annex B and C of the Circular based on the results of the one-time cleansing activities; • submit the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) for the recognition of items not previously recorded in the books of accounts but found in station and the derecognition of those not found in the station or those missing/non-existing PPEs without available records of accountability; and • incorporate the disclosures prescribed in Section 9 of COA Circular 2020-006 for fair presentation in the financial statements. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor submit to the Audit Team a request for the derecognition of the Non-Existing/Missing PPEs with the attached documents required in Section 8.3 of COA Circular 2020-006 for proper review and evaluation.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality of Pila was able to complete all the procedures and activities of the one-time cleansing during the CY 2023 and comparison of the PPE balances in the RPCPPE against the accounting records showed no variance. All the documentation prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the one-time cleansing was duly submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to: a. prepare schedule of aging of receivables and assess/evaluate the collectability of the noted dormant receivables by completing the ongoing update on the inventory of borrowers; and b. send final demand letters to the borrowers assessed to be still collectible. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. consider providing Allowance for Impairment for possible loss on the assessed/evaluated non-collectible receivables, in compliance with the pertinent provisions of IPSAS 29; and b. reclassify those assessed/evaluated non-collectible loans receivables to Other Receivables account to facilitate request for write-off and conduct necessary procedures to secure/produce all relevant documents enumerated under Item 8.1 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 to support the filing of request for authority to write-off of noted dormant receivables from the Commission on Audit.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant initially assessed that receivables totaling P1,079,775.00 are uncollectible. For the remaining amount, Management is still in the process of updating the inventory of borrowers and will create a loan reconstruction program for the benefit of the Municipality and the borrowers. No action was taken by Management on sending final demand letters. The Municipal Accountant already prepared the necessary adjusting entries to provide Allowance for Impairment for the assessed/evaluated non-collectible receivables amounting to P10,079,775.00 through Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) No. 2024-05-001804 dated May 22, 2024. These accounts pertain to deceased borrowers which will be applied for request for write-off upon completion of the necessary documents pursuant to COA Circular No. 2023-008.
observations.reason: Not Updating of inventory of borrowers still ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: a. Appraisal and Disposal Committee to facilitate the immediate disposal of the noted unserviceable property, in accordance with the existing rules and regulations; and b. Municipal Accountant to draw a JEV to drop from the books of accounts the sold/condemned property upon disposal.
observations.managementAction: The Disposal Committee facilitated the immediate disposal of the noted unserviceable property in accordance with the existing rules and regulations. The Municipality was able to identify unserviceable properties and prepared the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Municipal Treasurer to prepare an action plan and conduct collection campaign program to improve collection of tax delinquencies; and b. Municipal Assessor and Municipal Treasurer to place controls that will compensate the noted systems limitation and control weakness.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasury Office in partnership with the Office of the Municipal Assessor launched and implemented various tax caravans and tax campaign to improve collection of tax delinquencies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: a. Municipal Assessor, Municipal Treasurer and Information Technology Officer, in coordination with the service provider, to study and evaluate the possible enhancement of the system to include a reporting facility that will generate accurate, complete and reliable reports of Receivables and Revenues as of a given date; and b. Municipal Assessor and Municipal Treasurer to place controls that will compensate the noted systems limitation and control weakness.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Assessor, Municipal Treasurer and Information Technology Officer coordinated with the service provider for the possible enhancement of the system. The Municipal Assessor and Municipal Treasurer assigned a personnel in charge of generating the reports which is done every month end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to: a. effectively and judiciously plan the PPAs to be included in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) by considering its feasibility and necessity and which could be strictly implemented for a given timeframe; b. ensure that the LDRRMFIP is prepared in the prescribed format with specific timeline for each PPAs as a monitoring tool to its proper and timely implementation; c. maintain close coordination with the Chairman of the Bids and Awards Committee for the proper and timely procurement of planned PPAs under the LDRRMF; and d. maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF to achieve the objective of strengthening the capacity of the Municipality to build a disaster resilient community and enhance the disaster preparedness and response capabilities.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office prepared the LDRRMFIP in accordance with the prescribed format. The MDRRMC conducted a meeting to remind members to make timely requests to implement PPAs that they proposed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC - Municipal Accountant to correctly classify the outstanding balance of the long-term loans payable by presenting the portion of loans due for settlement within twelve months from the current year in the amount of P313,118.36 as part of the current liabilities and the remaining balance of P619,962.48 which is due for settlement beyond one year as non-current liabilities in the Statement of Financial Position.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken on the audit recommendation. The OIC - Municipal Accountant explained that the adjustment for the account was overlooked and the current and non-current portion of the loan was not properly presented in the Financial Statements.
observations.reason: The OIC - Municipal Accountant explained that the adjustment for the account was overlooked and the current and non-current portion of the loan was not properly presented in the Financial Statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned personnel to prepare the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances for In-Kind Donations; Report on the Receipt and Utilization of Cash Donations; and Report on the Receipt and Distribution/Issuance of In-Kind Donations and furnish the same to the National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (NDRRMC), through the Office of the Civil Defense (OCD), copy furnished the Audit Team, within ten working days after the end of each quarter, in accordance with COA Circular Nos. 2014-002 and 2020-009.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the Management.
observations.reason: The list of donations was not prepared due to non-coordination between the offices responsible in the receiving and issuance of donated goods.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended thatthe Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Human Resources Office to review and analyze the employees assigned in the
Accounting and GSO including their current duties and responsibilities and workload;
and if necessary, hire additional staff to distribute the excess workload of other
personnel;
b. Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical inventory and submit a
properly accomplish RPCPPE to the Audit Team within the prescribed period in accordance
with Section 124, Chapter 7 of the MNGAS for LGUS, Volume I; and
c. OIC-Municipal Accountant and the Designated - MGSO to periodically
reconcile their records to provide the correct PPE balances pursuant to
Sections 5.12 and 6.3of COACircularNo. 2020-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the OIC - Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile, on a monthly basis, the
Cash-In-Bank account balances per Ledger and per Cashbooks, and to
immediately makethenecessary adjustments/correction.

We recommended thatthe Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. MSWDO and other concerned offices to provide and submit the lacking
supporting documents specifically the valid IDs and the softcopies of the
photographs of the beneficiaries during the actual receipt of the financial
assistance totheOIC -Municipal Accountant; and
b. OIC - Municipal Accountant to fast track the review of the submitted
payroll and supporting documents and record the liquidations in the
appropriate books.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Designated-MGSO to
directly submit to the Office of the Auditor the copies of POs and their
supporting documents within five working days after its execution/issuance,
and prepare and furnish the corresponding Notices of Deliveries within 24
hours after acceptance of delivery of goods and services, in compliance with
COA Circular Nos. 2009-001 and 2009-002, and Annex 29 of the Manual on the
New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units
(LGUs), Volume II.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer
and other concerned offices to immediately collect the receivables from the
borrowers by continuously sending demand letters and strictly implementing
other available remedies to increase the collection and the funds available that
can be used to deliver basic services to its constituents pursuant to Section 2
of PD No. 1445.

We recommended thatthe Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Designated - MGSO to purchase the essential and listed supplies and
materials for the first quarter through the preparation of one-time
preparation of PR;

b. concerned officials to procure common-use supplies from the DBM-PS in
accordance with Section 53.5of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 toavail of
the most advantageous price. If the items to be procured are not available
thereat, attach a certification that the items are not available at the DBM-
PS;and

c. concerned officials exercise due diligence in the implementation of
programs/projects/activities (PPAs) by considering the timing of
procurement, priority and necessity of the PPAs to be implemented to
avoid wastage of government funds and take full advantage of the
available benefits offered.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: a. MGSO, in coordination with the Inventory Committee and Local School Board, to prepare and submit a duly accomplished RPCPPE for the PPE accounts under General Fund and Special Education Fund; b. OIC - Municipal Accountant and the MGSO to update and reconcile their records, thoroughly evaluate and analyze the causes of discrepancies for all the PPE accounts and make necessary adjustments in their records to ascertain the accuracy of the PPE balances; and c. the Municipal Mayor require the MGSO to observe proper maintenance of the Property Cards (PCs) per category of PPE for all the properties of the Municipality. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the OIC - Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to reclassify the small tangible assets totaling P877,907.83 from PPE to its appropriate inventory account, if not yet issued, or prior period adjustment account, if already issued, to ensure the fair presentation of the accounts in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction:
The Designated – MGSO informed the Audit Team that the Municipal Mayor, through the issuance of an Executive Order, will create an Inventory Committee who will conduct the physical inventory and one-time cleansing of PPE accounts.

In addition, the OIC-Municipal Accountant and the Designated-MGSO will periodically reconcile their records to provide the correct PPE balances.
observations.reason: Lack of manpower and excessive workload.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to maintain complete set of cashbooks for all its depository accounts, for proper recording and monitoring of all bank transactions involved in the cash in bank accounts and to thereafter reconcile with the records of the OIC - Municipal Accountant, in compliance with Sections 13 and 27, Chapter 3 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I. We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer monitor the checks that were not yet encashed and notify the payee of the existence of the check at least one month before it becomes stale. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC - Municipal Accountant to reconcile the difference of P13,183.88 between the Interest Income presented in the Statement of Cash Flows and Statement of Financial Performance and make necessary adjustments, if warranted, for fair presentation of the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The OIC - Municipal Accountant reconciled the difference of P13,183.88 and submitted to the Audit Team a written explanation. The Municipal Treasurer notified the payee of the existence of the check for payment of delivered goods and services. Stale check with check no. 1876622 dated March 16, 2020 payable to Triwil Herbs and Beans Cafe in the net amount of P2,468.10 was replaced with check no. 1912866 dated December 31, 2020. The stale checks were remitted to the BIR for the treatment of the taxes withheld.
observations.reason: There was still a variance between the cashbook and the General Ledger which was attributable to non-periodic reconciliation between the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant caused by heavy workload, lack of personnel and voluminous transactions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: a. Municipal Assessor to prepare and submit to the Municipal Treasurer, on or before the thirty-first (31st) day of December each year, the Municipal Treasurer’s duly certified list containing a list of all taxpayers whose properties have been newly assessed or reassessed and the amount collectible for the year to furnish the OIC - Municipal Accountant; b. Municipal Treasurer to update their List of Assessed Real Property Tax and submit duly certified list showing the name of the taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year; and c. OIC - Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) at the beginning of the year by debiting RPT Receivable/SET Receivable and crediting Deferred RPT Income/Deferred SET Income based on the duly certified list provided from the Municipal Treasurer for fair presentation of the accounts in the Financial Statements. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the OIC - Municipal Accountant to reconcile the RPT/SET Receivables accounts with the Municipal Treasurer’s list of RPT/SET delinquencies and make necessary adjustments.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Assessor submitted an updated list of taxpayers generated from the computer system which was already linked with the current system being used by the Municipal Treasurer. The Municipal Treasurer updated their List of Assessed Real Property Tax and submitted duly certified list showing the name of the taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year. The OIC - Municipal Accountant set up the accounts through JEV No. 2021-12-005127 and 2022-01-00002 dated December 31, 2021 and January 1, 2022, respectively.
observations.reason: Lack of manpower and excessive workload in both offices.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the OIC - Municipal Accountant to: a. continue issuing demand letters for the settlement of outstanding receivables; b. set-up the corresponding audit Allowance for Impairment in the books of accounts based on the loan receivables assessed collectability of the long outstanding receivables; and c. gather the necessary documents required under COA Circular No. 2016-005 to be able to file a request for write-off of dormant accounts and if granted, derecognize the related dormant uncollectible accounts.
observations.managementAction: The OIC - Municipal Accountant issued demand letters dated May 11, 2021 to the borrowers through the Barangay Captains.
observations.reason: No action was taken on the audit recommendation to set-up the Allowance for Impairment and to file request for write-off due to lack of documents and sufficient evidence that will prove the collectability of the receivables.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MGSO to maintain Stock Cards (SCs) and conduct actual inventory of all its Inventory accounts and prepare and submit the complete RPCI to the Audit Team as required by Sections 119 and 124, Chapter 7 of the MNGAS for LGU, Volume I. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MGSO and OIC - Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records and make necessary adjustments, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: The Designated-MGSO prepared and submitted the RPCPPE for the PPE accounts under General Fund and Special Education Fund but with incomplete details such as condition of the properties. The OIC - Municipal Accountant and Designated MGSO partially reconciled the Accounting Records and RPCPPE. The OIC - Municipal Accountant and Designated-MGSO reconciled the Accounting Records and RPCI.
observations.reason: Lack of manpower, time and excessive workload in both offices.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC - Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to correct the noted deficiency in the General and Trust Fund books by effecting the necessary adjustments to correct erroneous classification of accounts.
observations.managementAction: The OIC - Municipal Accountant reclassified the accounts through JEV No. 2021-06-000148 dated June 23, 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal General Services Officer and Municipal Assessor to expedite the processing of TCTs and facilitate the processing of TCTs for the lots to protect the interest of the Municipality and to ascertain the reliability of the recorded land account.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor instructed the concerned officials to facilitate the processing of TCTs. The concerned official has already requested the corresponding RTC clearances or Certificate of Technical Descriptions. The Municipal Mayor consulted a lawyer for the preparation and requirements needed in court proceedings. Currently, the Municipal Assessor is working on the Technical Descriptions of the lots.
observations.reason: The processing of TCTs was delayed due to requirements such as RTC Clearances and Technical Descriptions. Moreover, four lots has to undergo court proceedings.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: a. prepare and submit within the prescribed period a copy of the Annual Budget of all ongoing and to be implemented government PPAs as mandated by Section 122 of PD No. 1445 and Section 2.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004; b. Municipal Engineer to prepare and maintain a monthly monitoring report containing all the PPAs and necessary details of all the PPAs for ready reference in updating the Project Status and in preparation of the quarterly reports as required under Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 2013-004; and c. notify the Audit Team within ten days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate project signboards are already posted pursuant to Section 3.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004.
observations.managementAction: The OIC - Municipal Accountant submitted the comprehensive list of all ongoing and to be implemented PPAs as mandated by Section 122 of PD No. 1445 and Section 2.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004. The Municipal Engineer submitted the Quarterly Report on the Government PPAs (GPPAs) indicating the ongoing projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to: a. explain/justify the delays in the procurements process, and henceforth, strictly observe the allowable/recommended periods or time for action on specific procurement activities as outlined in Annex C of the 2016 R-IRR of RA No. 9184; b. carefully examine the submitted documents of the bidders to ensure their compliance and eligibility; and c. ensure the preparation and submission of the bid evaluation report and post-qualification report as part of the documentary requirements for the disbursements. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned OIC - Municipal Accountant to ensure completeness of supporting documents, especially the warranty securities posted by the contractors before the final payment of the infrastructure projects; the Municipal Engineer to follow-up the concerned contractors to submit "As Built Plans" and the BAC to ensure OSS standard format.
observations.managementAction: The BAC Chairman submitted a written explanation/justification together with other documents pertaining to the noted deficiencies in the bid evaluation and post-qualification. The BAC carefully examined the submitted documents of the bidders against the checklist of supporting documents. The BAC complied with the audit recommendation. The OIC - Municipal Accountant strictly checks the completeness of the supporting documents specifically the warranty securities. The Municipal Engineer submitted the "As-built Plans" to the Audit Team. The BAC required the bidders to comply with the OSS standard format. The Designated MGSO submitted the POs and Contracts to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC - Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to immediately submit all the required documents to support the disbursements charged against the BGCM in compliance with Section 4 of PD No. 1445, Section 4 of GPPB Circular No. 1-2020 and COA Circular No. 2012-001. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC Secretariat to comply with the posting requirements for information and transparency, in compliance with Section 4 of the LBC No. 125 and Section 3.6 of GPPB Circular No. 2020-01.
observations.managementAction: The OIC - Municipal Accountant submitted the lacking documents to the Audit Team. The BAC Secretariat complied with the posting requirements for information and transparency.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Head of MSWD and OIC - Municipal Accountant to: a. request copy of signed MOA with the DSWD and furnish copy to the Audit Team; b. submit photos of all the beneficiaries during the actual receipt of the subsidy and photocopy of the IDs with specimen signatures of the beneficiaries to support the liquidation of the cash advances; c. validate the SAC forms of the 11 beneficiaries who replied "No" on the confirmation and submit photocopy of the IDs and pictures of the beneficiaries to the Audit Team or conduct an in-depth investigation; and d. explain in writing the causes of the delay in the distribution of the SAP and devise effective mechanisms to expedite distribution.
observations.managementAction: The MOA with the DSWD FO was submitted to the Audit Team. The MSWDO and other concerned offices already submitted to OIC-Municipal Accountant the supporting documents for Ayuda 1 and Ayuda 2. Cash Advances for Ayuda 1 were already liquidated per various JEVs dated May 23 and 26, 2022 in the total amount of P48,946,000.00 while liquidation for cash advances under Ayuda 2 is ongoing. The MSWD personnel in charged of the SAP submitted explanation to the Audit Team. The Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office validated the SAC forms, IDs and pictures of the beneficiaries and provided certified true copies.
observations.reason: Delay in liquidation was due to difficulty in providing supporting documents caused by lack of screening of qualified beneficiaries, some recipients in the Master List were not physically present during the distribution and proper documentation was not strictly imposed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Municipal Engineer and all concerned officials to: a. effectively plan the priority development programs, projects and activities to be included in the AIP by considering their feasibility and necessity; and b. maximize the utilization of the 20 percent DF to achieve desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes.
observations.managementAction: According to the MPDC, most of the PPAs under the 20 percent DF for the CYs 2021 and 2022 were either bidded or awarded, ongoing implementation and some were already completed.
observations.reason: The plan is still not effectively carried out due to lapses in the detailed engineering activities and poor planning.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (LDRRMC) to review and update the LDRRMFIP by including all the programs/projects/activities (PPAs) of the previous years’ unexpended balances and any supplemental budget in compliance with Section 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality will include the unexpended balances in the current year’s supplemental budget as the need arises, otherwise, PPAs of the previous years’ unexpended balances will be included in the 2023 AIP.
observations.reason: The DRRM Council failed to include the unexpended balances since they are preparing Supplemental AIP for the projects to be funded by the unexpended balances transferred into the Special Trust Fund whenever necessary.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned officials in-charge of preparing the Purchase Request and Purchase Order to properly fill-up the said documents which includes the complete product characteristics and/or performance requirements and other details required thereto. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC and the Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) to furnish the Auditor with copies of contracts and purchase orders with their supporting documents within five working days from their perfection or issuance in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001.

The BAC Secretariat complied with the posting requirements. The Designated MGSO submitted the POs and Contracts to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: Some POs and contracts were submitted beyond the reglementary period.

the accounts intheFinancial Statements.

to be able to file a request for write off and if granted, derecognize the

c. to prepare and submit Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) for unserviceable property and equipment that are no longer needed for their proper disposal. Waste Material Report (WMR) shall likewise be prepared to document the disposal made. Based on the prepared IIRUP and WRM, require the Municipal Accountant should be required to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to drop the property from the books of accounts; and
d. submit the report taken regarding the missing property.
observations.managementAction:
Completion of the Inventory Report is still in process.
Initiated the initial disposal of unserviceable PPE.
IIRUP was prepared by the MGSO.
observations.reason: Not Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 1, Page No.25.

observations.managementAction: Demand letters were issued and personal follow ups were conducted, thus a total of P71,650.00 was collected in CY 2018.

observations.recommendation:

observations.managementAction:

observations.reason: There was increased tax delinquency due to additional delinquent taxpayers despite measures taken.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor take action to address the cash deficit. We also recommended that Trust Funds be utilized only for specific purpose for which it was intended as provided under Section 309 (b) of RA No. 7160 and Section 94 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I. We likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to closely monitor the cash balance of Trust Fund and enjoin the Municipal Treasurer to refrain from certifying funds availability when no cash is available, otherwise, actions shall be taken against those responsible for the existence of cash deficit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Local Municipal Treasurer to address the discrepancy between their records on the Cash- in Local Treasury account and the Cashbook and make the necessary adjustments. Henceforth, regular reconciliation of the Cash- in Local Treasury balances between the General Ledger and the Cashbook should be undertaken for all funds in compliance with Section 181 (c) of GAAM Volume I.
observations.managementAction: Adjustments were made on June 28, 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to enforce collection of the RPT by granting incentives or relief to delinquent taxpayers thru compromise agreement, with the authority of the Sangguniang Bayan, otherwise, avail of the remedies provided under Section 256 of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor created Tax Caravan and Tax Research Teams to intensify the collections of business and real property taxes (RPT). Together with other units/offices, the Teams coordinated with the concerned Barangay Officials for the collections of RPT from delinquent taxpayers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office (MSWDO) to draw appropriate plans by identifying and consolidating the items needed in the implementation of the Supplemental Feeding Program and consider procurement through public bidding, except in highly exceptional cases and justified by the conditions set forth in Rule XVI of RA No. 9184. We further recommended that the concerned management officials stop the practice of procuring goods through cash advance and transferring the cash advances from one accountable officer to another pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and refrain from transferring cash advances from one accountable officer to another.

All necessary documents were attached in the liquidation report. The management already stop the practice of procuring goods through cash advance and transferring the cash advances from one accountable officer to another.
